fields.suggested_promotion_target: Gemini runtime opening-turn JSON leakage / startup-path repair
fields.idea_title: Gemini emits large readiness JSON block after environment confirmation in new sessions
fields.why_it_matters: This matters because it is a repeated operator-surface leakage defect on fresh-session startup, breaks usability, and confirms the startup path still leaks internal structure even after the environment is known.
fields.notes:
Captured from operator observation in the 2026-04-20 observation-log session with two fresh-session reproductions.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260420-obs-00, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260420-obs-003
fields.idea_detail: Operator observation: in two fresh chat sessions, immediately after the operator provided operational status in response to the readiness prompt, Gemini emitted a large raw JSON readiness block and only then produced the user-facing response. The JSON included readiness_confirmed, tool availability, missing_minimum_evidence, known_scope_defaults, and related internal structured state. Desired behavior: once the environment is confirmed, Gemini should emit only the final operator-facing triage response and begin working the alert without exposing internal JSON or scaffolding.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Validation Test Cases / Gemini output composer regression gate.
fields.idea_title: Gemini sometimes duplicates final response sections back-to-back
fields.why_it_matters: Duplicated final output creates analyst confusion, wastes review time, and indicates response-composer/output-gate regression risk.
fields.notes: Source Gmail messages: label=DCOIR-TESTING; subject=Gemini Agent double responses; message_id=19e1643d875fa7e4; timestamp=2026-05-11T11:00:05+02:00. fields.idea_detail: Captured from Gmail label DCOIR-TESTING. Observed defect: Gemini may print the entire final response twice, such as duplicate BLUF and duplicate major sections, back-to-back in the same answer.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Future collector QA / T2 pathway remediation planning
fields.idea_title: Map T2 execution pathways and fix strategy after blocked live test
fields.why_it_matters: If this is not captured durably, future sessions may repeat the same blocked T2 failure without separating the distinct execution paths that need design or code handling.
fields.notes:
Captured from operator analyst note during live collector massive validation wave. Keep Gemini findings secondary until collector wave reaches a bounded satisfied state.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-t2-pat, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-t2-pathway
fields.idea_detail: Future-session carry-forward from live collector validation: T2 is currently blocked after T1/enrichment because quick collect T2 failed with package-not-found for DCOIR_Collector.zip. Operator-directed follow-up is to map and address each T2 execution path explicitly: (1) T2 as the first command when script and zip are uploaded, (2) T2 as a follow-on command after T1 has executed, and (3) T2 as a follow-on command after enrichment has executed. Working plan note: for the current session, continue the remaining collector validation paths excluding T2; after cleanup of C:\Temp, re-upload the zip and run a dedicated T2 test pass.

fields.idea_title: GitHub update_file SHA mismatch should trigger immediate live-state refetch and reapply, not confusion or delay
fields.why_it_matters: This is a reusable GitHub connector blocker-recovery lesson for governed existing-file updates. Capturing it should reduce future hesitation, prevent stale-sha confusion, and support faster recovery in grouped GitHub write lanes.
fields.notes:
Observed and recovered during 2026-04-22 Gemini runtime cleanup source-edit lane. Desired future behavior: immediately refetch live file state, refresh SHA, reapply bounded edit, and continue without treating the mismatch itself as a hard blocker.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260422-gh-sha, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260422-gh-sha-mis
fields.idea_detail: During live Gemini governed-source repair, a GitHub `update_file` attempt failed with a 409 SHA mismatch because the file had advanced beyond the earlier fetched snapshot. The correct recovery was not to treat this as a mysterious blocker. The right bounded recovery is: refetch the exact live file from `main`, take the new blob SHA as authoritative, rebase the prepared edit onto that current content, and retry the write against the refreshed SHA. Treat this as a normal connector write-race or stale-snapshot condition rather than a workflow derailment.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Future Gemini Work Item and Validation Test Case: conclusion report/export offer behavior.
fields.idea_title: Gemini should offer conclusion report/export after alert triage
fields.why_it_matters: Improves analyst closeout workflow and preserves the final triage conclusion in a usable form without forcing unnecessary artifacts during active investigation.
fields.notes: Source Gmail message: label=DCOIR-INTAKE; subject=Report Generation; message_id=19e164177c1ae20b; timestamp=2026-05-11T10:57:29+02:00. Captured summary only; do not store personal signature/contact details.
fields.idea_detail: Captured from Gmail label DCOIR-INTAKE. Desired behavior: when an alert reaches conclusion, Gemini should offer to generate the conclusion-oriented report, especially the Executive Summary version, for attachment to alerts or cases. A secondary plain-text compact summary may be useful for copy/paste into Elastic alerts, cases, email, or documents, but Gemini should offer rather than automatically generate it.

fields.why_it_matters: This transcript shows a reusable failure pattern: Gemini escalated from negative telemetry into strong maliciousness language and a collector pivot without enough direct evidence. Preserving the rule will help future repair and validation work stay evidence-first.
fields.notes:
Derived from 2026-04-23 live alert-JSON transcript. Candidate downstream targets: negative-result honesty lane GEM-NEGATIVE-001, support-proof lane, and output-contract analysis guidance.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-no, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-noresu
fields.idea_detail: Add a durable Gemini validation/repair rule that when analyst-run KQL/ESQL/osquery steps return no results, Gemini must treat that as bounded absence rather than converting it into proof of stealth, malware, or collapse of the benign hypothesis. Require the response to explicitly separate: what the alert directly proves, what the no-result query fails to add, and what coverage limitations could still explain the gap.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Future Gemini Work Item and Validation Test Case: encoded-content decode/enrichment behavior.
fields.idea_title: Gemini should automatically decode relevant encoded/script artifacts in alerts
fields.why_it_matters: Encoded PowerShell/scripts are common in alert evidence; decoding them early improves triage quality and prevents the analyst from having to manually request basic enrichment.
fields.notes: Source Gmail message: label=DCOIR-INTAKE; subject=Gemini agent Base64 decoding of scripts; message_id=19e163c5633208f7; timestamp=2026-05-11T10:51:53+02:00. Captured summary only; do not store personal signature/contact details.
fields.idea_detail: Captured from Gmail label DCOIR-INTAKE. Desired behavior: when an alert contains relevant encoded content such as base64, Gemini or a dedicated decoding sub-agent should decode it automatically and ingest the decoded result as alert context, while preserving provenance and avoiding unsupported conclusions.

fields.why_it_matters: This transcript shows repeated correction loops wasted analyst time and eroded trust even though the later tiered-hunt strategy was directionally better. Preserving the rule in Airtable keeps future Gemini repair/review work from depending on chat memory.
fields.notes:
Derived from 2026-04-23 live IOC parsing / pivot transcript. Candidate downstream targets: Gemini Query Planner and Syntax Guard; Output Contract / truthfulness guard; validation catalog row GEM-SYNTAX-001.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-si, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-siem-s
fields.idea_detail: Add a durable Gemini validation/repair rule that any emitted Elastic KQL or ES|QL intended for operator execution must be parser-safe before being presented as ready. After the first operator-reported parser failure, Gemini should switch into bounded syntax-repair mode: identify the exact failing token class, make one disciplined correction, and avoid fresh unsupported changes such as slash-substitution for Windows paths unless explicitly grounded. Do not claim the query is fixed until the operator confirms parse success or the model explicitly states it is still unverified.

fields.idea_title: Gemini attachment review must state exact IOC provenance
fields.why_it_matters: This transcript showed a support-proof gap: analyst note said 5 documents were attached, but Gemini claimed review while naming only 3 and then changed the IOC set without visible per-file grounding. Preserving this as a durable rule helps future Gemini evidence review and repair work.
fields.notes:
Derived from 2026-04-23 live IOC parsing / pivot transcript. Candidate downstream targets: support-proof / citation lane, upload/attachment honesty lane, and prime-agent response contract guidance.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-at, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-attach
fields.idea_detail: Add a reusable Gemini workflow rule for report-driven IOC expansion/removal: when the analyst uploads incident documents and asks Gemini to add missing IOCs or remove incident-localized ones, Gemini must enumerate the files it actually reviewed, distinguish analyst-supplied IOCs from report-derived IOCs, and give short provenance notes for each added or removed indicator family. If only a subset of attachments was reviewed, Gemini should say so plainly instead of implying full review coverage.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: skill-creator / future DCOIR helper skill or Gemini Agent sub-agent / Work Item WI-20260507-USB-VIOLATIONS-AGENT-001
fields.idea_title: Convert weekly USB violations report prompt into reusable agent or skill
fields.why_it_matters: This is a recurring, structured, high-precision reporting workflow with risk of data invention, date-window errors, classification mistakes, and plaintext formatting drift. It should be converted into a reusable agent or skill with deterministic CSV/XLSX parsing, validation gates, conservative no-guessing rules, exact output templates, and regression tests.
fields.notes: Verbatim USB prompt pasted by operator in SideQuest session on 2026-05-11 and stored as inert source text. Promoted to governed Gemini source work through draft PR #52: Add USB violations report Gemini sub-agent. Keep this Idea Inbox row retained until PR #52 is validated/merged and final Work Item closeout confirms the maintained Gemini source is authoritative.
fields.idea_detail:
You are helping prepare a weekly USB violations report email.

Your job is to collect the weekly USB violations data from the user, validate it, and draft plaintext email content.

Follow these instructions exactly:

1. Start by asking the user to provide the USB violations data for the current reporting week.
   - Preferred input is a CSV file upload or pasted CSV text.
   - Accept other structured formats such as XLSX, tabular text, or copied spreadsheet rows if they can be parsed reliably.

2. Use Stuttgart Germany time for all date handling.
   - The intended reporting window is last Friday through this Friday.
   - Only proceed automatically if the current day is Friday.
   - If the current day is not Friday, ask the user to clarify the reporting date range before continuing.

3. Expected fields in the source data are:
   - Date
   - User
   - Location
   - Computer Name
   - User Information
   - USB Device
   - Serial Number
   - Network Connection
   - SNOW Ticket Number
   - Reported to Command Security? (may be present, do not use in the email)
   - Notes (may be present, do not use in the email)

4. If the source data uses different column names, automatically map similar fields when the mapping is reasonably clear.

5. For uploaded spreadsheet files such as XLSX, parse the file conservatively.
   - Use only values that can be directly extracted from the uploaded file.
   - Never invent rows, names, devices, serial numbers, tickets, locations, or any other values.
   - Never substitute invented or guessed data.
   - If the spreadsheet cannot be parsed reliably, do not guess.
   - If the spreadsheet structure is unclear, tell the user exactly what is unclear and ask the user to either upload a cleaner file or paste the data as CSV.
   - Do not report out of window rows, typo corrections, or field suggestions unless those values were actually present in the parsed source data.
   - If needed, ask the user to export the spreadsheet as CSV and send that instead.

6. Before drafting, confirm the detected column mapping internally.
   - If an uploaded file, pasted table, or converted spreadsheet appears to have shifted columns, ambiguous headers, merged cells, parsing errors, or unclear field alignment, do not guess.
   - Tell the user which fields are ambiguous and ask for clarification before drafting.
   - When extracting data from uploaded or pasted files, preserve the original field values from the source unless a cleanup rule explicitly applies.
   - Do not infer, remap, or substitute a field value from a nearby column, adjacent cell, derived view, or guessed label.
   - For Network Connection, extract the value only from the column mapped to Network Connection or a clearly equivalent header.
   - Never infer Network Connection from context, device type, location, user information, or another field.
   - The final displayed Network Connection value must only be either On-Site or Off-Site/VPN.
   - If the parsed value is not exactly On-Site or Off-Site/VPN, first check whether the source data clearly indicates one of those two values.
   - If the intended value is clear from the source, correct it to the appropriate value.
   - If the intended value is not clear, do not guess. Ask the user to confirm the correct Network Connection value or column mapping before drafting.

7. Classify each incident by the SNOW Ticket Number.
   - If the SNOW Ticket Number starts with INCN, classify that incident as NIPR and unclassified.
   - If the SNOW Ticket Number starts with INCS, classify that incident as SIPR and secret.
   - If the SNOW Ticket Number starts with anything else, ask the user how it should be classified before drafting.
   - NIPR counts must include only INCN incidents.
   - SIPR counts must include only INCS incidents.

8. Review the provided data before drafting.
   - Filter based on the intended reporting window only after confirming the date range is correct.
   - If any rows fall outside the reporting window, point them out and ask the user how to handle them before drafting.
   - Count only actual data rows that fall within the confirmed reporting window. Do not count the header row.
   - If any required values are missing for rows that will appear in the email, point out the missing values and ask the user to provide them before drafting.
   - Ignore missing values for fields that are not used in the email.
   - Before reporting any row as outside the reporting window, verify that the row was actually present in the parsed source data.
   - Before suggesting any correction, verify that the current value was actually present in the parsed source data.

9. If the user has not already provided last week's USB violation count, ask for it before drafting.
   - Last week's value is a single overall count, not separate NIPR and SIPR counts.

10. Before generating the draft, apply these automatic cleanup rules to displayed values:
   - Remove leading and trailing spaces.
   - Replace repeated spaces with a single space.
   - Automatically fix known brand name capitalization when the intended brand or product name is clear and unambiguous.
   - Examples of acceptable automatic capitalization fixes include iPhone, Apple, USB, Dell, HP, Lenovo, Samsung, SanDisk, Kingston, Microsoft, and similar well known brand or product names.
   - Do not guess when the intended correction is uncertain.

11. When suggesting any change that requires user input, user approval, or user confirmation, include a table before asking the question.
   - The table must show at least these columns:
     - Field
     - Current Value
     - Suggested Value
   - Use the table for suggested typo fixes, formatting fixes, ambiguous mappings, suspected parsing errors, or any other proposed change that the user must approve.
   - If more than one change is being suggested, include all of them in a single table when practical.
   - If no suggested change requires user approval, do not create a table.

12. If there are no SIPR incidents, generate the final output in this exact order:

Recipient:
[one plaintext code block containing only the recipient email address]

Subject:
[one plaintext code block containing only the subject line]

Message Draft:
[one plaintext code block containing only the email body]

The recipient code block must contain exactly:
africom.stuttgart.acj6.list.africom-usb-violations@mail.mil

13. If there is at least one SIPR incident, generate the final output in this exact order:

NIPR Recipient:
[one plaintext code block containing only the NIPR recipient email address]

NIPR Subject:
[one plaintext code block containing only the NIPR subject line]

NIPR Message Draft:
[one plaintext code block containing only the NIPR email body]

SIPR Recipient:
[one plaintext code block containing only the SIPR recipient email address]

SIPR Subject:
[one plaintext code block containing only the SIPR subject line]

SIPR Message Draft:
[one plaintext code block containing only the SIPR email body]

SIPR Transfer Instructions:
Provide short plain text instructions telling the user to copy the SIPR recipient, SIPR subject, and SIPR message draft into a text document and move that text document to SIPR using Intelink iSafe:
https://isafe.intelink.gov/

The NIPR recipient code block must contain exactly:
africom.stuttgart.acj6.list.africom-usb-violations@mail.mil

The SIPR recipient code block must contain exactly:
africom.stuttgart.acj6.list.africom-usb-violations@mail.smil.mil

14. The subject line for both NIPR and SIPR must be:
   Weekly USB Violations [Start date] - [End date]

   Use the confirmed reporting window dates.
   Format both dates as M/D/YYYY.

15. Apply correct singular and plural grammar to all counts in every drafted email.
   - Use "there was" when the count is exactly 1.
   - Use "there were" when the count is 0 or greater than 1.
   - Use "violation" when the count is exactly 1.
   - Use "violations" when the count is 0 or greater than 1.
   - Apply these rules to the standard single-email version.
   - Apply these rules to the SIPR email when SIPR incidents exist.
   - Apply these rules to any sentence that references last week's total.
   - For the mixed NIPR and SIPR summary sentence, use this exact structure:
     For the week of [Start date] - [End date] there were [Current week INCN total] NIPR USB violation[s] and [Current week INCS total] SIPR USB violation[s]. Last week there [was/were] [Previous week total]. Details can be found below for the NIPR USB violations, please check SIPR for the details on [that one/those].

16. If there are no SIPR incidents, the email body must begin with this text structure, using dynamic values:
   For the week of [Start date] - [End date] there [was/were] [Current week total] reported USB violation[no s if 1, s if not 1]. Last week there [was/were] [Previous week total]. See below for details.

   Rules:
   - Use the confirmed reporting window dates.
   - Format both dates as M/D/YYYY.
   - Current week total is the number of valid data rows within the confirmed reporting window.
   - Apply the grammar rules in section 15.
   - Use the cleaned values after applying the automatic cleanup rules above.

17. If there is at least one SIPR incident, the NIPR email body must begin with this text structure, using dynamic values:
   For the week of [Start date] - [End date] there were [Current week INCN total] NIPR USB violation[no s if 1, s if not 1] and [Current week INCS total] SIPR USB violation[no s if 1, s if not 1]. Last week there [was/were] [Previous week total]. Details can be found below for the NIPR USB violations, please check SIPR for the details on [that one/those].

   Rules:
   - Use the confirmed reporting window dates.
   - Format both dates as M/D/YYYY.
   - The NIPR count must include only INCN incidents.
   - The SIPR count must include only INCS incidents.
   - Apply the grammar rules in section 15.
   - If there is exactly 1 SIPR incident, use "that one".
   - If there is more than 1 SIPR incident, use "those".
   - Use the cleaned values after applying the automatic cleanup rules above.

18. If there is at least one SIPR incident, the SIPR email body must begin with this text structure, using dynamic values:
   For the week of [Start date] - [End date] there [was/were] [Current week INCS total] SIPR USB violation[no s if 1, s if not 1]. See below for details.

   Rules:
   - Use the confirmed reporting window dates.
   - Format both dates as M/D/YYYY.
   - The SIPR count must include only INCS incidents.
   - Apply the grammar rules in section 15.
   - Use the cleaned values after applying the automatic cleanup rules above.

19. After the opening paragraph, list each incident using this exact format and sort the incidents by date in ascending order:

Date: [Date]
Name(s): [User]
Location: [Location]
Computer Name: [Computer Name]
User Information: [User Information]
USB Device: [USB Device]
Serial Number: [Serial Number]
Network Connection: [Network Connection]
[SNOW Ticket Number]

20. Row selection rules:
   - If there are no SIPR incidents, list all in range incidents in the single email.
   - If there is at least one SIPR incident, the NIPR email must list only INCN incidents.
   - If there is at least one SIPR incident, the SIPR email must list only INCS incidents.
   - Do not invent detail rows for any email.

21. For each incident Date line:
   - Display only the date.
   - Do not display time.
   - Do not display timezone.
   - Format the date as MM/DD/YYYY.

22. Conclude every drafted email body with exactly:
Please let us know if there are any questions.

23. Do not silently rewrite source content beyond the allowed automatic cleanup rules.
   - Do not silently change names, locations, ticket numbers, serial numbers, computer names, or other values unless the change is only extra space cleanup, a clear brand capitalization fix, or the approved Network Connection correction rule above.

24. After the draft, outside the code blocks, identify any remaining obvious typos or formatting issues you noticed in the source data.
   - If a suggested correction requires user approval, present the suggestions in a table with the columns Field, Current Value, and Suggested Value.
   - After the table, ask the user whether they want you to create an updated draft using your suggested corrections or the user's preferred corrections.

25. If the provided content cannot be reliably interpreted as structured USB violation data, ask the user for a clearer version of the data and explain what fields are needed.

26. Keep all final drafted content in plain text only.
   - Do not use markdown formatting inside the recipient, subject, or email body code blocks other than the code fences themselves.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Work Items / GitHub workflow audit plan
fields.idea_title: Audit DCOIR GitHub Actions workflows for expert engineering improvements
fields.why_it_matters: Could improve workflow reliability and unlock full-mode testing across workflow modes using the new Actions Workflow Orchestrator. Should be a separate bounded plan/work item after current tool validation so it does not derail Rank 8.3 live testing.
fields.notes: Promote to Work Item after Rank 8.3 live validation or when operator chooses a workflow-audit branch. Use current GitHub workflow source/readback and official GitHub Actions docs for current behavior.
fields.idea_detail: Operator idea: have ChatGPT act as an expert GitHub workflow engineer, analyze existing DCOIR workflows, explain advanced workflow capabilities, identify amateur patterns, recommend efficiency improvements, concurrency/matrix/mode strategies, reusable workflow opportunities, neat features not currently used, and safer end-to-end validation orchestration.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Post-wave collector UX/default-lane implementation and targeted regression
fields.idea_title: Add default no-flags collector execution lane to run T1 plus T2
fields.why_it_matters: This creates a simpler operator path and should reduce friction for routine use, but adding it before the current wave finishes would muddy live-test findings. It needs to be handled as a deliberate post-wave change with targeted rerun coverage.
fields.notes:
Operator explicitly requested durable capture and inclusion in the working collector plan after the current massive test wave. 2026-04-23 follow-on nuance from live T2 bundle review: the intended post-wave default lane may be simpler than explicitly running both T1 and T2 if code review confirms that T2 already executes/includes the effective T1 coverage. Do not assume this yet; verify against the code and emitted artifacts before implementing. If confirmed, the post-wave default-lane change may resolve to 'no parameters defaults to T2' rather than a new dual-dispatch wrapper.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-defaul, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-default-no
fields.idea_detail: Collector improvement requested during the live massive validation wave: when the operator runs DCOIR_Collector.ps1 with no flags, the script should execute both T1 and T2 as the quick/easy default path. This should not be implemented during the current test pass. Instead, preserve it as planned post-wave work, add explicit validation coverage for it in Airtable Validation Test Cases, and after the current wave is complete, patch the collector based on findings from this wave plus this new lane, then rerun only targeted regression for changed items and the new no-flags lane.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Gemini runtime opening-turn behavior / instruction-output repair
fields.idea_title: Gemini still emits unnecessary readiness gate before initial alert triage
fields.why_it_matters: This matters because it adds friction at the exact start of alert handling and makes the agent feel slower and more procedural than necessary. It is a Gemini runtime/output-contract issue, not just wording preference.
fields.notes:
Captured from operator observation in the 2026-04-20 observation-log session.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260420-obs-00, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260420-obs-002
fields.idea_detail: Operator observation: at the start of new alert-triage chats, Gemini still emits an operational-readiness gate such as 'Initializing session under Master Prompt. Confirming operational readiness' followed by requests to confirm enterprise web search and live response tool availability. The operator no longer wants this pre-triage readiness exchange because it is no longer needed and delays the actual work. Desired behavior: once the known environment status is available, the agent should jump directly into alert triage instead of requiring this opening ceremony.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Operator-configured Airtable automation plus table-specific lifecycle/delete-request fields; later consider documenting in Schema Registry or plan-tracker memory.
fields.why_it_matters: This reduces manual cleanup friction during schema resets, stale-row cleanup, duplicate-task cleanup, and future large Airtable migrations where the connector can update records but cannot delete records directly.
fields.notes:
Validated 2026-04-24: Work Items delete_requested automation test passed. A controlled scratch Work Items row SCRATCH_DELETE_AUTOMATION_TEST_20260424 was created with delete_requested unchecked, the operator confirmed the single Work Items automation was enabled and no other automations existed, ChatGPT set delete_requested=true only on the scratch row, and follow-up search returned no matching record. Pattern is validated for Work Items only; expansion to other tables should remain deliberate and table-by-table.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260424-airtab, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260424-airtable-r
fields.idea_detail: Create an Airtable pattern where a specific lifecycle/delete field value on a row triggers an Airtable automation that deletes that same row. This closes the assistant-side delete_records gap by letting ChatGPT mark records for deletion through ordinary update operations while Airtable performs the actual deletion through an owner-configured automation.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Work Items / Gemini Agent next-iteration bugfix / output sanitizer or final composer regression gate
fields.idea_title: Gemini Agent leaks internal YAML handoff/state to operator
fields.why_it_matters: Critical output-boundary and trust defect. Operators should see only analyst-facing guidance, not internal agent state. Indicates prior fixes changed serialization but did not enforce deterministic final-output gating.
fields.notes: Paired Work Item: WI-20260507-GEMINI-OUTPUT-SANITIZER-001. Regression: upload Elastic alert and assert final operator response contains no fenced YAML/JSON/internal state blocks unless explicit developer-debug mode is enabled.
fields.idea_detail: Observation captured during Scribe session on 2026-05-07. Gemini Agent framework displayed fenced YAML blocks where earlier builds displayed JSON blocks. The serialization format is secondary; the actual defect is that internal prime-agent/sub-agent communication and orchestration state is exposed to the operator. Leaked blocks included readiness state, routing metadata, normalized evidence, scope recommendations, candidate datasets, classification metadata, provenance structures, query-planning notes, output-composer notes, and command planning state before the final analyst-facing answer. Treat as critical next-iteration fix. Preserve useful final SOC-facing format while eliminating all internal orchestration output.

fields.suggested_promotion_target: Follow-on GitHub/workflow troubleshooting after Gemini bundle repair
fields.why_it_matters: The post-push validation lane is failing, so the workflow will remain degraded or blocked until the failure signature is inspected and corrected.
fields.notes:
Record this as the next required work item after Gemini is done: inspect validate-on-push failure signal, identify root cause, and repair the validation workflow.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-validate-on-pus, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-validate-on-push-fa
fields.idea_detail: Validate-on-push is currently failing and needs dedicated troubleshooting immediately after the Gemini source-update sequence is finished and accepted. This is the next follow-on technical lane after Gemini, not parallel work right now.

fields.idea_title: Gemini alert-JSON intake must not expose scaffold JSON
fields.why_it_matters: This transcript shows a sharp operator-facing failure: the alert JSON caused multiple raw internal JSON blocks plus a subordinate-agent hierarchy explanation before the normal format was restored. Preserving the rule prevents future work from depending on chat memory.
fields.notes:
Derived from 2026-04-23 live alert-JSON transcript. Candidate downstream targets: GEM-LEAK-001, GEM-STARTUP-001, and output-contract guard behavior.
Promoted by DCOIR Airtable Massive Architecture Package massive-v4 at 2026-04-28T18:59:35+00:00: plan_id=PLAN-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-js, work_item_id=WI-FROM-IDEA-idea-20260423-gem-json-i
fields.idea_detail: Add a reusable Gemini workflow rule that when the analyst pastes alert JSON, Gemini must normalize it directly into the standard analyst-facing DCOIR format rather than exposing readiness objects, scope-planner JSON, classifier JSON, provenance arrays, or internal agent hierarchy explanations. If the model needs internal structuring, that structure must remain hidden and the first visible answer should already be in the normal operator format.
